meta.name: The Letter of Jeremiah
meta.copyright: New Revised Standard Version Bible, copyright © 1989 National Council of the Churches of Christ in the United States of America. Used by permission. All rights reserved worldwide.

text.Base: A copy of a letter that Jeremiah sent to those who were to be taken as captives to Babylon by the king of the Babylonians, to give them the message that God had commanded him.
text.KJV: 1:1 A copy of an Epistle which Ieremie sent vnto them which were to be led captiues into Babylon, by the king of the Babylonians, to certifie them as it was commanded him of God.
text.DRV: 1:1 For the sins that you have sinned before God, you shall be led away captive into Babylon by Nabuchodonosor the king of Babylon.
text.NABRE: 1:1 IV. Letter of Jeremiah Chapter 6 - A copy of the letter which Jeremiah sent to those led captive to Babylon by the king of the Babylonians, to tell them what God had commanded him: For the sins you committed before God, you are being led captive to Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar, king of the Babylonians.

text.Base: Because of the sins that you have committed before God, you will be taken to Babylon as captives by Nebuchadnezzar, king of the Babylonians.
text.KJV: 1:2 Because of þ sinnes which ye haue committed before God, ye shall be led away captiues vnto Babylon by Nabuchodonosor king of the Babylonians.
text.DRV: 1:2 Being entered therefore into Babylon, you shall be there many years, and long times even unto seven generations: and after this I will bring you forth from thence with peace.
text.NABRE: 1:2 When you reach Babylon you will be there many years, a long time—seven generations; after that I will bring you back from there in peace.

text.Base: “Now in Babylon you will see gods made of silver and gold and wood, which people carry on their shoulders, and which cause the nations to fear.”
text.KJV: 1:3 So when ye be come vnto Babylon, ye shal remaine there many yeeres, and for a long season, namely seuen generations: and after that I will bring you away peaceably from thence.
text.DRV: 1:3 But now you shall see in Babylon gods of gold, and of silver, and of stone, and of wood to be carried upon shoulders, shewing fear to the Gentiles.
text.NABRE: 1:3 And now in Babylon you will see gods of silver and gold and wood, carried shoulder high, to cast fear upon the nations.

text.Base: “So beware of becoming at all like the foreigners or of letting fear for these gods take hold of you when you see the multitude before and behind them worshiping them.”
text.KJV: 1:4 Now shal ye see in Babylon gods of siluer, and of gold, and of wood, borne vpon shoulders, which cause the nations to feare.
text.DRV: 1:4 Beware therefore lest you also be like to the doing of strangers, and you be afraid, and fear take you in them.
text.NABRE: 1:4 Take care that you yourselves do not become like these foreigners and let not such fear possess you.

text.Base: “But say in your heart, ‘It is you, O Lord, whom we must worship.’”
text.KJV: 1:5 Beware therefore that yee in no wise be like to strangers, neither be yee afraid of them, when yee see the multitude before them, and behinde them, worshipping them.
text.DRV: 1:5 Seeing therefore the multitude adoring behind, and before, say you in your hearts: Thou oughtest to be adored o Lord.
text.NABRE: 1:5 When you see the crowd before them and behind worshiping them, say in your hearts, “You, Lord, are the one to be worshiped!”

text.Base: “For my angel is with you, and he is watching over your lives.”
text.KJV: 1:6 But say yee in your hearts, O Lord, we must worship thee.
text.DRV: 1:6 For mine Angel is with you: and myself will ask account of your souls.
text.NABRE: 1:6 For my angel is with you, and he will keep watch on you.

text.Base: “For their tongues are smoothed by the artisan, and they themselves are overlaid with gold and silver; but they are a fraud and cannot speak.”
text.KJV: 1:8 As for their tongue, it is polished by the workeman, and they themselues are guilded and laid ouer with siluer, yet are they but false and cannot speake.
text.DRV: 1:8 And as to a virgin that loveth ornaments: so taking gold their gods are forged.
text.NABRE: 1:8 People bring gold, as though for a girl fond of dressing up,

text.Base: “They even take some of the clothing from the gods and put it on their own wives and children.”
text.KJV: 1:11 Yea they will giue thereof to the common harlots, and decke them as men with garments gods of siluer, and gods of gold, and wood.
text.DRV: 1:11 But these are not delivered from the rust, and the moth.
text.NABRE: 1:11 Though they are wrapped in purple clothing, they are not safe from rust and corrosion.

text.Base: “He has a dagger in his right hand, and an ax; but he cannot deliver himself from war and robbers.”
text.KJV: 1:15 Hee hath also in his right hand a dagger, and an axe: but cannot deliuer himselfe from warre and theeues.
text.DRV: 1:15 Therefore fear them not. For as a man's vessel being broken is made unprofitable: such also are their gods:
text.NABRE: 1:15 As useless as a broken pot

text.Base: “Having no feet, they are carried on the shoulders of others, revealing to humankind their worthlessness.”
text.KJV: 1:26 They are borne vpon shoulders, hauing no feete, whereby they declare vnto men that they be nothing worth.
text.DRV: 1:26 Therefore if they fall to the ground, they rise not up of themselves, nor if a man set him upright, shall he stand by himself, but as to dead men their gifts shall be set before them.
text.NABRE: 1:26 because, if they fall to the ground, the worshipers must pick them up. They neither move of themselves if one sets them upright, nor come upright if they are tipped over; offerings are set out for them as for the dead.

text.Base: Better then is the righteous person who has no idols, for he will be far from reproach.
text.KJV: 1:73 Better therefore is the iust man that hath none idoles: for he shall be farre from reproach.
